1: O LORD, God who avenges — El neqamot (God of vengeance/retribution) is the specific divine title invoked here. Neqamah is not personal revenge but judicial retribution — the punishment that restores the moral order disrupted by injustice. The same term appears in Deut 32:35 ("vengeance is mine and retribution" — quoted in Rom 12:19 and Heb 10:30) and in Isa 34:8 ("the day of YHWH's vengeance"). The invocation of this specific divine title establishes the psalm's framework: this is a judicial lament, appealing to YHWH in his role as the cosmic judge who is obligated by his own character to correct injustice. "Arise and shine forth" — the theophany formula calling YHWH to act visibly.
2: Rise up, Judge of the earth — shophet ha-aretz (Judge of the earth) is the title Abraham uses in his intercession for Sodom (Gen 18:25: "shall not the Judge of all the earth do right?"). The invocation of YHWH as universal judge — not merely Israel's patron deity but the judge of the entire earth — is the theological ground for the complaint that follows: the wicked who oppress the vulnerable are not just violating human norms but are defying the moral order of the cosmic judge. "Pay back to the proud what they deserve" (hashev gemul al ge'im) — the covenant guarantee of proportional retribution (Obad 1:15; Prov 11:31).
3: How long, LORD, will the wicked — how long will the wicked rejoice in triumph? — the doubled "how long" is the signature temporal question of the lament psalms (Ps 6:3; 13:1-2; 35:17; 74:10; 79:5; 80:4; 82:2; 89:46). The question is not philosophical (how long philosophically might evil exist?) but practical and covenant-grounded: given that YHWH is the God who avenges (v1) and the Judge of the earth (v2), how long will the current situation persist without his intervention? The double repetition creates the urgency and the felt endlessness of the situation from the psalmist's perspective.
4: They spew proud words without end; all who do evil brag without restraint — the specific sin of verbal arrogance: yabbi'u yedabberu ataq (they pour out/spew arrogant/hard things). Ataq (arrogant, presumptuous) is the word for speech that treats YHWH's authority as irrelevant. The imagery of pouring/spewing suggests the uncontrolled overflow of arrogance rather than calculated speech. "Without restraint" (yitammeru) — they boast without any internal check or external accountability. The absence of shame is itself a symptom of the practical atheism that v7 makes explicit.
5: They crush your people, LORD, and oppress those who belong to you — the shift from verbal arrogance (v4) to physical oppression. "Your people" and "your inheritance" (ammekha... nahalatkha) are the covenant terms for Israel as YHWH's possession — the ones who belong specifically to him by covenant (Deut 4:20; 9:29). The oppressors are not merely committing social crimes; they are violating YHWH's property. This framing makes the divine responsibility explicit: YHWH's honor requires that he defend his own people from those who crush them.
6: They murder the widow and the foreigner and kill the fatherless — the three categories of the legally unprotected: the widow (almanah), the foreigner (ger), and the fatherless/orphan (yatom). These three categories appear together repeatedly in Deuteronomy (10:18; 14:29; 16:11; 24:17-21; 26:12-13; 27:19) and the prophets (Isa 1:17, 23; 10:2; Jer 7:6; 22:3; Ezek 22:7; Zech 7:10; Mal 3:5) as the specific persons whom YHWH commands Israel to protect. They have no legal advocate, no family patron, no one to take their case. Murdering them — using violence specifically against those who cannot defend themselves — is the archetype of covenant injustice.
7: And they say, 'The LORD never sees it; the God of Jacob pays no attention' — the theological rationale for the oppression: practical atheism, the belief that YHWH's perception is limited or that he does not concern himself with human affairs. This is exactly the same charge as Ps 64:5 ("who will see us?"), Ps 10:11 ("God has forgotten; he covers his face and never sees"), and Ezek 8:12 ("the LORD does not see us; the LORD has forsaken the land"). The practical atheist is not necessarily a theoretical atheist — they may affirm YHWH's existence while denying that his perception and moral governance extends to their specific actions. This belief licenses all manner of oppression.
8: Grasp this, you most senseless among the people — fools, when will you become wise? — the psalm turns from lament to confrontation, addressing the oppressors directly with wisdom vocabulary. Ba'arim (senseless ones, like animals) and kesilim (fools) — the same pairing as in Ps 92:6. The question "when will you become wise?" (matai taskilu) is a rhetorical challenge: wisdom is still available, but the fools are choosing not to acquire it. The wisdom tradition's argument is that the practical atheism of v7 is not just morally wrong but epistemically stupid — it makes a claim about YHWH's perception that the creation itself refutes.
9: The one who made the ear — does he not hear? The one who formed the eye — does he not see? — the creation-based epistemological argument against practical atheism: the Creator necessarily possesses the capacities he creates. The argument is a fortiori: if human beings (finite creatures) can hear and see, how much more must the Creator of hearing and seeing be able to hear and see? This argument is structurally similar to Paul's argument in Rom 1:20 (creation reveals the invisible attributes of God) and appears in various forms in Second Temple wisdom literature. The argument does not prove divine intervention; it proves divine perception — the prerequisite for the just governance the psalmist is requesting.
10: The one who corrects the nations — will he not call them to account? The one who teaches all people — expanding the argument from perception to governance. YHWH who corrects (meyasser, disciplines, chastens) the nations and who teaches (hamelamed) all people has both the knowledge and the governing authority over human affairs. The nations-scope moves beyond Israel: YHWH's governance is universal, not merely tribal. The rhetorical questions build to a climax: if YHWH perceives (v9) and governs (v10), then the practical atheism of v7 is both false and self-destructive.
11: The LORD knows what human beings think — that their thoughts are nothing but vapor — the content of YHWH's knowledge of human cognition: the machshevot adam (thoughts of humanity) are hevel (vapor, vanity, futility — the keynote word of Ecclesiastes). Paul quotes this verse in 1 Cor 3:20 ("the Lord knows that the thoughts of the wise are futile") in the context of warning the Corinthian community against human wisdom that excludes divine wisdom. The divine knowledge of human thought-vanity is both a judgment on practical atheism and a source of comfort to the oppressed: the oppressors' clever plans for covering their tracks are known to YHWH as vapor.
12: Blessed is the one you discipline, LORD, and instruct from your teaching — the beatitude of divine correction. The one under YHWH's yasar (discipline, correction) and lamad (instruction, teaching through Torah) is called blessed (ashre). This is the same paradox as Prov 3:11-12 ("do not despise the LORD's discipline... for the LORD disciplines those he loves, as a father the son he delights in") and Heb 12:5-11 (which quotes Prov 3:11-12 and applies it to the Christian community under suffering). The discipline that feels like punishment is actually the mechanism of formation: the disciplined one is being made wiser rather than merely punished.
13: Giving him rest in days of trouble until a pit is dug for the wicked — the temporal structure of the blessing. The hashqet (rest, relief, quietness) is given specifically during the days of trouble — not after the trouble ends but within it. This is the same structure as Ps 91:15 ("I will be with him in his trouble"). The "until" clause places the wicked's end as a certainty on the horizon: the pit they are digging for others will become their own grave (compare Ps 7:15; 57:6). The rest the righteous receive is not the absence of trouble but the quality of YHWH's presence within it.
14: The LORD will not abandon his people or desert his inheritance — the covenant guarantee stated as a absolute negative. The same covenant faithfulness invoked in Deut 31:6 (Moses' promise to Joshua: "he will never leave you nor forsake you"), 1 Sam 12:22 ("for his great name's sake YHWH will not reject his people"), and Ps 37:28 ("the LORD loves the just and will not forsake his faithful ones"). The double negative construction (lo ya'azov... velo ya'azov — not abandon... not desert) is emphatic: the covenant relationship has no provision for divine desertion of the covenant people, regardless of circumstances.
15: Surely justice will return to the righteous, and all who are upright in heart will pursue it — the eschatological confidence that the present moral disorder is not permanent. "Justice will return" (ad-tzedek yashov mishpat) — the current situation in which justice is corrupted or absent will be reversed. The "upright in heart" (yishrei lev) — those whose inward moral reality matches their outward profession — will actively pursue (ahareyv, follow after) justice when it returns. The verse anticipates a future situation in which righteous governance is restored and the community of the genuinely upright can act on it.
16: Who will stand up for me against the wicked? Who will take my side against evildoers? — the rhetorical question that anticipates the answer: no human advocate is available; only YHWH can serve this role. The social system that should provide legal advocates (family patrons, tribal elders, royal courts) has been captured by the corrupt throne of v20. The psalm's appeal is therefore not to human institutions but directly to the divine advocate. This is the same move as Job's appeal to a "redeemer" (go'el) who will advocate for him before YHWH (Job 19:25) — when the human legal system fails, the divine one must step in.
17: If the LORD had not been my help, I would very soon have lain in the silence of death — the personal testimony of near-death rescue. "The silence of death" (dumah) — the same word as Ps 62:1's dumiyyah but here with the specific meaning of the silence of Sheol, the realm of the dead (Ps 115:17: "the dead do not praise the LORD, nor those who go down to silence"). The psalmist testifies that without YHWH's specific intervention they would now be in Sheol. This testimony is the personal corollary to the theological claim of v14: YHWH does not abandon his people, and the evidence is this specific survival.
18: When I felt my foot giving way, your steadfast love, LORD, held me steady — hesed as the practical force that prevents falling. The foot giving way (motah ragli) is the same stumbling-prevention motif as Ps 91:12 (the angels who hold you up so your foot does not strike against a stone). Here it is hesed itself — covenant loyalty embodied as a steadying force — that prevents the fall. The psalmist did not slip into Sheol (v17) because YHWH's hesed actively held them in place. This is the experiential confirmation of the psalm's opening theology: the one who has made YHWH their refuge is held steady by covenant love even at the edge of the pit.
19: When anxious thoughts multiply inside me, your comfort brings delight to my soul — the interior psychology of the lament. Sar'appai (my anxious thoughts, my distracting/conflicted thoughts) multiplying internally is the experience of the mind overwhelmed by threats. The word is rare — appearing also in Ps 139:23 and Job 4:13 — and carries the sense of branching, splitting thoughts that cannot converge on a single response. YHWH's tanhumim (consolations, comforts) counter the multiplication of anxiety with delight (shaa'ashu nafshi, literally: they play with/delight my soul). The divine comforts are not rational arguments but the lived experience of divine nearness.
20: Can a corrupt throne have any fellowship with you — one that enacts harm through its own laws? — the specific indictment of legal injustice: the throne that yotzer amal al-chok (creates trouble/harm by means of statute/law). This is the worst form of wickedness because it uses the apparatus of justice to perpetrate injustice — the law is used as the instrument of oppression. The prophets consistently identify this as the highest form of covenant violation: Isa 10:1-4 ("woe to those who make unjust laws and issue oppressive decrees"), Mic 3:9-11 (leaders who despise justice and pervert what is right). The corrupt throne cannot have chevruth (fellowship, association) with YHWH because his character is its direct opposite.
21: They join forces against the lives of the righteous and condemn the innocent to death — the coalition (yagoddu, they band together/conspire) against the legally innocent. The legal system that should protect the innocent is being used to condemn them — the perversion of the judicial process that Proverbs, the prophets, and the Torah consistently identify as the special object of divine wrath. "Condemn the innocent to death" (vedam naki yarshi'u) — declaring the innocent guilty (the legal opposite of justice) produces the death sentence. This is the archetype of the miscarriage of justice that the Psalter and the prophets demand YHWH avenge.
22: But the LORD is my strong fortress, and my God is the rock where I take shelter — the personal confession of faith that answers the corrupt institutions of vv 20-21. The political system has been captured by the corrupt throne; therefore the psalmist cannot appeal to earthly institutions. But YHWH as misgav (fortress, high place) and tsur machsi (rock of my shelter) is the refuge that human corruption cannot compromise. The fortress vocabulary returns from v1's invocation and provides the personal counterpart to the theological claim: the God of vengeance (v1) is also the personal fortress (v22).
23: He will pay them back for their sin and destroy them for their evil; the LORD our God will wipe them out — the psalm's closing declaration of divine retribution. The three-fold statement of divine judgment — repaying (yashev), destroying (yatzmitem), wiping out (yatzmitem YHWH elohenu) — uses the same covenant vocabulary of complete judgment that appears in the Deuteronomic curses (Deut 28:20-25) and the prophetic oracles against the nations. The final phrase "YHWH our God" reclaims the covenant relationship that the corrupt throne denied: the God who judges the wicked is specifically the covenant God of the community that was oppressed.
